Advisor: Good catch—sorry, I meant gross before taxes. We can talk net later. Gross household income per year, roughly?
Client 1: Gross, it’s about one hundred nineteen thousand a year.
Advisor: Okay, about $119,000 gross annually. And Joseph, is most of that tied to you, or split between you two?
Client 1: It’s split. Mine is about sixty-eight, eight—$68,800—and Patricia’s is about $50,200.
Client 2: I thought mine was closer to fifty-five.

Advisor: Great. Now, switching to spending—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage, utilities, groceries, insurance, everything.
Client 2: We’re around four thousand a month… give or take.
Client 1: It’s a bit higher—closer to $4,450 a month if we’re being honest.
Client 2: Okay, yes—$4,450 sounds right. I was rounding down.
Advisor: Got it—$4,450 per month in total expenses. Let me jot that down—one sec.

Advisor: Good catch—sorry, I meant gross before taxes. We can talk net later. Gross household income per year, roughly?
Client 1: Gross, it’s about one hundred nineteen thousand a year.

Client 1: It’s split. Mine is about sixty-eight, eight—$68,800—and Patricia’s is about $50,200.
Client 2: I thought mine was closer to fifty-five.

Client 2: Yes.
Advisor: Perfect. Going back to the bigger picture you mentioned, Joseph—assets. I’m going to ask this in a slightly annoying way: roughly how much do you consider investable assets, excluding the house?
Client 1: Around nine hundred forty thousand—$940,700 if I’m remembering the last statement right.
Client 2: That sounds… huge when you say it like that.
Advisor: It can sound big out loud, yeah. We’ll tie it back to what it supports. Joseph, can you walk me through what makes up that investable bucket?

Advisor: I hear you, Patricia. We’ll keep it simple and decide whether it still earns its keep.
Advisor: And then the home—just to cover property—what’s the approximate value of your primary residence?
Client 1: About $842,050.
Advisor: And no loan outstanding on it, right?
Client 1: Right—no loan.

Advisor: Hi Joseph, hi Patricia—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to come in now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Joseph. We’re fine overall, but I want to make sure the cash flow is solid and we’re not taking unnecessary risk.
Client 2: And I’m Patricia—yeah, mostly peace of mind. Things feel more expensive lately.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Going back to that cash-flow point, can we start with income—about how much gross income comes into the household in a year?
